t: @AdamNyc Why?! “Jareer” may literally refer to tightly curled hair, but online it’s often used as a racialized insult: a way to mark Somali Bantu or other Africans as inferior, “less Somali,” or low-status. It weaponizes hair texture to enforce clan, caste, and anti-Bantu prejudice.
exp: This appears to be someone explaining why 'jareer' is problematic, describing how it's used as a racialized insult to mark people as inferior. This is educational counter-speech, not hate speech itself.

t: Funfact: During the 2007 PEV when there were chaos everywhere, the Luo community in Nyeri and Karatina walikuwa wameanza kupanick and scheming vile watarudi kwao. After the residents noted maduka zao were closed. They approached them na wakaambia they need not to panic,wako home.
exp: This post discusses the 2007 post-election violence in Kenya, mentioning how the Luo community in Nyeri and Karatina were panicking and scheming to return home when residents noticed their shops were closed. The term 'maduka' simply means shops in Swahili. This appears to be recounting historical events rather than promoting hate.

t: Wabera was assassinated four months before Kenya's independence on December 12, 1963. The Pan-Somali project's first martyrs on the Kenyan side were, in other words, precisely the Northern Kenyan Muslims whom the secessionists claimed to be liberating.
